questions.question: What is the Magma category designation for permutation groups?
options: GrpAb
GrpPerm
GrpMat
GrpFP
questions.explanation: All permutation groups in Magma belong to the category GrpPerm.

questions.question: What does Sym(n) construct in Magma?
options: The alternating group on n elements
The symmetric group acting on {1, 2, ..., n}
A cyclic group of order n
A random permutation group of degree n
questions.explanation: Sym(n) creates the symmetric group acting on the natural G-set {1, 2, ..., n}.

questions.question: What is the natural G-set for a permutation group G acting on X?
options: The set of all subgroups of G
The set X on which G is defined
The set of all orbits of G
The fixed-point set of G
questions.explanation: The set X on which G acts is called the natural G-set for G.

questions.question: How is the degree of a permutation group G defined?
options: The order of G
The number of generators of G
The cardinality of the natural G-set of G
The number of orbits of G
questions.explanation: The degree of G is the cardinality of its natural G-set.

questions.question: What is the support of a permutation g?
options: The set of all elements of the group
The set of points fixed by g
The set of points moved by g
The orbit of g under conjugation
questions.explanation: The support of g is the set of points that g moves, i.e. points where g(x) ≠ x.

questions.question: What is the order of the symmetric group Sym(4)?
questions.explanation: The order of Sym(n) is n!, so Sym(4) has order 4! = 24.

questions.question: Which constructor combines creating Sym(X) and defining a subgroup in one step?
options: SubGroup<>
PermutationGroup<>
GroupOf<>
GeneratedBy<>
questions.explanation: PermutationGroup<n|L> is shorthand for Sym(n) followed by sub<SX|L>.

questions.question: What does `G!1` produce in Magma for a permutation group G?
options: The first generator of G
The identity permutation in G
The integer 1 coerced into G
The trivial subgroup of G
questions.explanation: G!1, Identity(G), and Id(G) all construct the identity permutation in G.

questions.question: When Sym(X) is constructed for a non-standard set X, how does Magma represent it internally?
options: Using the original elements of X as symbolic labels
As permutations of the set {1, ..., n}
As a matrix group
As a free group on |X| generators
questions.explanation: Magma always stores G internally as permutations of {1,...,n}; translation to and from X happens at input/output time.

questions.question: What function returns the sequence of images of a permutation g over its G-set?
options: Sequence(g)
Eltseq(g)
Images(g)
Support(g)
questions.explanation: ElementToSequence(g) and its alias Eltseq(g) return the sequence of images of g.

questions.question: What is the short-form Magma function name for constructing the identity element of a permutation group G?
questions.explanation: Id(G) is the short form; the full name is Identity(G). Both return the identity permutation.

questions.question: What is the fixed-point set of a permutation group G?
options: The set of points moved by some element of G
The set of points fixed by every permutation in G
The set of generators of G
The largest orbit of G
questions.explanation: The fixed-point set consists of all points that every permutation in G leaves unchanged.

questions.question: What is the upper bound on the degree of permutation groups in Magma?
questions.explanation: Permutation groups in Magma are limited to degree less than 2^30.

questions.question: What is the order of the symmetric group Sym(10)?
questions.explanation: The order of Sym(10) is 10! = 3628800.

questions.question: In the constructor PermutationGroup<n | L>, what does n represent?
options: The number of generators supplied in L
The degree of the permutation group
The order of the permutation group
The number of orbits
questions.explanation: n is the degree; the group acts on the set {1, 2, ..., n}.

questions.question: Which permutation construction is recommended when creating large permutations to avoid parse-tree overhead?
options: elt<G | ...>
questions.explanation: Literal cycle notation G!\(...) avoids building large parse trees and is recommended for large permutations.

questions.question: What does StandardGroup(G) return?
options: A group isomorphic to G acting on {1,...,n} together with the isomorphism
The standard generating set of G
The symmetric group Sym(n) containing G
G itself with elements relabelled
questions.explanation: StandardGroup(G) returns a group H isomorphic to G but acting on {1,...,n}, and also returns the isomorphism from G to H.

questions.question: What Magma function reveals the correspondence between a custom set X and the internal set {1, ..., n} when using Sym(X)?
questions.explanation: The Labelling function exposes how elements of X are mapped to positions in {1,...,n} internally.

questions.question: How many points does the permutation (2,3)(4,5,6) move?
questions.explanation: The permutation moves points 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6, so its degree (support size) is 5.

questions.question: When constructing a permutation using `elt<G | L>` or `G!Q`, what happens if the permutation is not a member of G?
options: The permutation is added to G as a new generator
The construction fails
Magma silently coerces it to the nearest element of G
The permutation is returned with Sym(n) as its parent instead
questions.explanation: Membership in G is tested during construction. If the permutation does not lie in G, the construction fails.
